summary: Evans breaks down the meta-skill of learning into four concrete, practicable sub-skills: identifying what you don't understand, having confidence in what you do know, asking good questions, and doing effective research. She argues that a growth mindset alone isn't sufficient—learning is itself a skill that requires deliberate practice. Drawing on her experience with self-directed math education, a pure math degree, and the Recurse Center, she shows how translating vague confusion into specific questions is the most critical skill. She reframes confusion as a positive signal that you're about to learn something new.
key_points: The most important learning skill is translating 'I'm confused' into specific, answerable questions—without this, you depend on others to structure your learning for you
Confidence in what you already know is just as important as identifying gaps—upgrading from 97% sure to 100% sure prevents wasted debugging time
Learning to learn is a skill developed through practice over years, from childhood math programs to PhD research to self-directed programming
Doing research effectively means knowing where information lives in your domain—man pages, books, mailing lists, source code—not just Googling
Confusion should be recognized as a positive signal that you're about to learn something, not a source of shame
money_quotes: Having a positive attitude is really important, but IMO by itself it's not enough to learn hard things. Learning is a skill which takes a lot of work to get better at.
This is the skill of translating 'I'm confused, I don't get it' to 'I have a specific question about X'.
There is a big difference between 'I'm 97% sure this is true' and 'I am 100% sure about this and I never need to question it again'.
I still find breaking down 'I'm confused about X' into specific questions about X takes work.

It seems a bit weird to put 'learn some math independently when you're 8' alongside 'do a PhD' but to me figuring out fractions on your own feels like the same kind of mental discipline that's useful as an adult researcher exploring new ideas.
Almost everything I do in my job day-to-day is something I learned on my own, and most of it is stuff that I learned recently (in the last 2-3 years).
If I'm confused, that's usually a good thing because it means I'm not stagnating.
key_insight: Learning hard things requires the concrete skill of decomposing vague confusion into specific, answerable questions—a skill that itself improves with deliberate practice.
